coredata.dc:title: In-situ growth of CeO2 on biofilms: Innovative nanoparticles for photothermal therapy & multi-pronged attack on Alzheimer’s disease
coredata.dc:creator: Chi, Mingyuan
Liu, Jichun
Li, Lianxin
Zhang, Yuewen
Xie, Meng
coredata.dc:description: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is complex and multifactorial, and its pathogenesis involves multiple factors and processes. This study pioneered the in situ growth of cerium oxide nanoparticles on macrophage membranes (Ce-RAW). Further, carbon quantum dots (CQD) were biomimetically modified by Ce-RAW, leading to the synthesis of a multifunctional nanocomposite (CQD-Ce-RAW). Within the framework of this research, CQD-Ce-RAW was strategically combined with photothermal therapy (PTT), aiming to achieve a more significant therapeutic effect. The macrophage membrane confers the system with anti-phagocytic and anti-inflammatory biological functions. More importantly, the ultra-small size of cerium oxide grown on the membrane acts as a reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenger and alleviates the degree of oxidative stress. Meanwhile, CQD as a photosensitizer helps dissociate amyloid-β (Aβ) aggregates and chelates excess copper ions, thus further inhibiting Aβ aggregation. Cell experiments showed that CQD-Ce-RAW combined with PTT could effectively degrade and inhibit the aggregation of Aβ, remove ROS, and improve cell survival rate. The results of in vivo photothermal experiments demonstrated that near-infrared light enhanced the efficiency of drug penetration through the blood-brain barrier and facilitated its accumulation in brain tissue. This comprehensive therapeutic approach can intervene in the disease progression from multiple pathways, providing a new prospect for treating AD.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multi-pathway therapy
Biomimetic nanocomposites
Amyloid-β
Reactive oxygen species
Copper ion
Alzheimer’s disease

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Regulation of Aβ42 decomposition and inhibition

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu province (BK20201419) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M701502).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CQD-Ce-RAW. HRTEM images of (a) CQD (scale bar = 5 nm), (b) RAW (scale bar = 200 nm), (c) CQD-Ce-RAW (scale bar = 100 nm), and (d) CeO2 on the membranes (curves represent nanocrystals and straight lines indicate the corresponding lattice spacing, scale bar = 5 nm). DLS assay of (e) zeta potential and (f) particle size of Ce-RAW, CQD, and CQD-Ce-RAW (n = 3). (g) Diameter changes of CQD-Ce-RAW placed in PBS and 10% DMEM for 5 days (n = 3). (h) FT-IR spectra of CQD. (i) UV-Vis-NIR of RAW, CQD, and CQD-Ce-RAW solutions. (j) Ce 3d XPS spectrum of CQD-Ce-RAW. Data are presented as mean ± SD. ***p < 0.001, n.s.: no significance.

Evaluation of photothermal properties of CQD-Ce-RAW when exposed to irradiation. (a) Photothermal curves of CQD-Ce-RAW solution with different concentrations were obtained by 808 nm laser irradiation. (b) Temperature variation of 0.2 mg/mL CQD-Ce-RAW during 5 cycles of heating and natural cooling under irradiation. (c) Cooling time data vs -ln θ and the corresponding linear fitting curves (2 W/cm2, 10 min).

Multiple enzyme activities and Cu2+ binding properties. The ability of CQD-RAW and CQD-Ce-RAW to (a) eliminate ·OH and (b) catalyze H2O2 were compared. (c) SOD enzyme-like activity of CQD-Ce-RAW at different concentrations. (d) The chelation rate of Cu2+ before and after coating CQD with Ce-RAW was determined using atomic absorption spectrometry (n = 3). Data are presented as mean ± SD. n.s.: no significance.

In vitro anti-Aβ42 activity of CQD-Ce-RAW. (a) ThT fluorescence values of Aβ42/Cu2+ solutions were measured for 7 consecutive days under different treatments. (b) Changes in ThT fluorescence of mixed solutions of CQD-Ce-RAW and Aβ42 fibrils before and after NIR irradiation. (c) TEM images and (e) fluorescence photographs of Aβ42/Cu2+ solutions at different incubation times in the presence or absence of CQD-Ce-RAW. Morphological changes of Aβ42 fibrils in the presence of CQD-Ce-RAW were analyzed by (d) TEM images and (f) fluorescence images before and after 808 nm laser irradiation (TEM scale = 500 nm, fluorescence microscope scale = 100 μm). Data are presented as mean ± SD. ***p < 0.001.

Cell viability, cellular uptake, and ROS scavenging capacity in vitro. (a) Cell viability of SH-SY5Y after 24 h incubation with different concentrations of CQD-Ce-RAW before and after irradiation. The data are presented as the means ± SD (n = 3). (b) CLSM images of SH-SY5Y cells ingesting CQD-Ce-RAW (scale bar = 50 μm). (c) Fluorescence images of Aβ42-induced ROS in SH-SY5Y cells under different treatment conditions (scale bar = 100 μm).

Rescue of Aβ42 damage to neuronal cells. (a) Cell viability of SH-SY5Y under treatment with different concentrations of Aβ42 (n=3). (b) Survival of SH-SY5Y after incubation with different concentrations of CQD-Ce-RAW with Aβ42/Cu2+ at 37°C, detected by MTT method (n=3). (c) Protective effects of CQD-Ce-RAW against Aβ42 fibers-induced SH-SY5Y cytotoxicity with or without NIR irradiation, respectively (n=3). (d) Fluorescence images of JC-1 monomers/aggregates obtained under Aβ42-induced SH-SY5Y cell conditions using CQD-Ce-RAW or its combined treatment with NIR laser (scale bar = 100 μm). Data presented as mean ± SD. ***p < 0.001.

Combined treatment enhances CQD-Ce-RAW delivery in brain tissue. (a) Schematic of transwell model. (b) Fluorescence images of transwell lower layer SH-SY5Y cells in the presence or absence of NIR irradiation (scale bar = 100 μm). (c) Results of brain temperature difference changes in Balb/c mice after each 808 nm laser irradiation. (d) Fluorescence signal intensity and (e) DID fluorescence imaging of brain tissues of mice 12 h after injection via tail vein. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.
(a) Preparation pathway of CQD-Ce-RAW. (b) Illustration of the multi-pathway therapeutic mechanism of CQD-Ce-RAW in combination with PTT for Alzheimer’s disease.

head.ce:title: In-situ growth of CeO2 on biofilms: Innovative nanoparticles for photothermal therapy & multi-pronged attack on Alzheimer’s disease

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is complex and multifactorial, and its pathogenesis involves multiple factors and processes. This study pioneered the in situ growth of cerium oxide nanoparticles on macrophage membranes (Ce-RAW). Further, carbon quantum dots (CQD) were biomimetically modified by Ce-RAW, leading to the synthesis of a multifunctional nanocomposite (CQD-Ce-RAW). Within the framework of this research, CQD-Ce-RAW was strategically combined with photothermal therapy (PTT), aiming to achieve a more significant therapeutic effect. The macrophage membrane confers the system with anti-phagocytic and anti-inflammatory biological functions. More importantly, the ultra-small size of cerium oxide grown on the membrane acts as a reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenger and alleviates the degree of oxidative stress. Meanwhile, CQD as a photosensitizer helps dissociate amyloid-β (Aβ) aggregates and chelates excess copper ions, thus further inhibiting Aβ aggregation. Cell experiments showed that CQD-Ce-RAW combined with PTT could effectively degrade and inhibit the aggregation of Aβ, remove ROS, and improve cell survival rate. The results of in vivo photothermal experiments demonstrated that near-infrared light enhanced the efficiency of drug penetration through the blood-brain barrier and facilitated its accumulation in brain tissue. This comprehensive therapeutic approach can intervene in the disease progression from multiple pathways, providing a new prospect for treating AD.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a multifaceted neurodegenerative disorder marked by gradual deterioration in cognitive abilities and alterations in personality and behavior [1]. So far, the exact pathogeny of AD is not fully clear, which leads to no radical treatment. Pharmacological and non-pharmacological treatments can be used to relieve symptoms and interfere with the progression of the disease [2]. However, the existing treatment methods are usually limited to a single pathological factor and fail to achieve the ideal clinical treatment effect [3]. It has been documented that numerous significant pathogenic mechanisms contribute to AD, encompassing amyloid aggregation, oxidative stress, and disruption of metal ion homeostasis [4–6]. In particular, atypical metabolism of amyloid precursor protein (APP) leads to abnormal aggregation of amyloid-β (Aβ) in the extracellular space of neuronal cells to form toxic aggregates [7]. Aβ aggregates further trigger a series of pathological events, such as oxidative stress and neuroinflammation. When redox metal ion levels are disturbed, especially copper ions (Cu2+), they interact with Aβ peptides to promote aggregation, leading to overproduction of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [8], which in turn exacerbates Aβ deposition [9]. High levels of Cu2+ in amyloid plaque deposits in the brains of AD patients have also been confirmed [10]. Given the interconnectedness of these pathologic factors, the development of drugs with multifunctionality holds great promise. By intervening in multiple pathological pathways simultaneously, to more comprehensive anti-AD. Currently, Aβ therapy is considered as a promising strategy, which aims to alleviate the process of Aβ accumulation and deposition, thereby reducing neuronal damage [11]. The two most prominent variants of the Aβ peptide are Aβ40 and Aβ42, of which Aβ42 is more susceptible to fibrillation and triggers cytotoxicity [12]. Therefore, Aβ42 was taken as the focus of this study. In order to achieve a “multi-pronged attack”, in addition to regulating Aβ aggregation, we also plan to chelate excess Cu2+ and reduce ROS levels in the brain. This comprehensive therapeutic approach is expected to make up for the shortcomings of existing treatments and produce a synergistic effect, thereby delaying the progression of AD.

In the field of AD treatment, researchers are trying to improve the delivery efficiency of drugs to the brain to obtain better therapeutic effects [13]. Several studies have shown that photothermal therapy (PTT) can enhance the permeability of the blood-brain barrier (BBB), thereby improving the efficiency of drug delivery [14]. In this study, a laser of 808 nm, a wavelength characterized by good tissue penetration, was used and applied in the therapeutic strategy of AD [15]. In addition, since Aβ in the patient’s brain has low thermal stability, photothermolysis can be used to absorb light energy and convert it into heat, increasing the local temperature to remove the formed Aβ fibers and mitigate the neurotoxicity it causes [16]. A key advantage of this approach lies in its ability to precisely target and remove these harmful protein deposits without causing damage to surrounding healthy tissues [17]. In recent years, the research field has witnessed the continuous development of photothermal agents, including two-dimensional transition metal-carbon/nitrides [18], magnetic nanoparticles [19], gold nanomaterials [20], and others. Carbon quantum dots (CQD) are ideal for photothermal applications due to their high photothermal conversion efficiency and thermal stability [21,22]. Under the excitation of a near-infrared (NIR) laser, CQD decomposes Aβ polymers with the help of thermal effects. Notably, the properties of CQD were further tailored by doping with nitrogen, endowing them with the ability to chelate Cu2+. This helps to inhibit the aggregation of Aβ peptides and reduce the fatal damage to neurons. Therefore, the combination of CQD with PTT is expected to mitigate the negative effects of the disease pathology and may become a central component of next-generation AD therapeutic strategies.

The combination of antioxidants and nanomaterials with photothermal properties to enhance efficacy is a new idea for the treatment of AD. Nanoenzymes are widely used in the fields of medicine and environmental protection due to their green, low-cost, and highly stable characteristics [23]. It has been reported that cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeO2), in which the +3 and +4 valence states of cerium can interconvert with each other as well as the presence of oxygen vacancies, exhibit excellent multiple enzyme-like activities, such as superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase (CAT) activities [24–26]. These properties have enabled it to exhibit excellent efficacy in eliminating ROS and thereby reducing apoptosis. It is worth mentioning that the catalytic activity of nanoenzymes is closely related to their size. CeO2 smaller than 5 nm possesses more active sites and thus stronger enzymatic activity due to its larger specific surface area [27]. Therefore, designing small-sized CeO2 as a ROS scavenger is preferred.

Although nanotherapeutics have made great progress, there are still some key problems, such as poor biological stability, limited blood circulation time, and inevitable immune response, which limit their application in vivo [28]. In order to compensate for these shortcomings, the method of using natural cell membrane camouflage has been widely studied and applied [29]. In this context, macrophage membrane (RAW) is particularly prominent due to its natural targeting property [30]. By encapsulating nanoparticles in the RAW, drugs can be delivered to the inflammatory areas in the brain of AD patients, thereby reducing the damage to normal cells and tissues and improving the therapeutic effect. Inspired by this, we first designed a multifunctional biomimetic nanocomposite using RAW as a template, and in-situ grown cerium oxide nanocrystals on it, and then coated on the CQD surface ( Scheme 1). This synthesis method has a dual advantage. On the one hand, CeO2 crystallized in situ on the membrane using the one-pot method has an ultra-small size, which optimizes the catalytic activity. On the other hand, the covered RAW confers source cell-related functions such as long cycling, immune evasion, and inflammation targeting of the system while improving the stability of the CQD. Taken together, under NIR irradiation, CQD-Ce-RAW can act as a photothermal agent, a metal ion chelator, and an antioxidant, realizing the goal of multi-pathway therapy and providing a potential solution for the development of more efficient AD treatments.

Cerium nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O), citric acid, urea, dihydroethidium (HE), thioflavin T (ThT), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. HEPES were obtained from Shanghai Saint-Bio Co., Ltd. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Iron sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), p-phthalic acid (TA), salicylic acid, copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT), and sodium phosphate dibasic dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Mouse leukemia cells of monocyte macrophage (RAW 264.7), Human neuroblastoma cells (SH-SY5Y), and Mouse brain microvascular endothelial cells (bEnd.3) were purchased from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were provided by HyClone Thermo Scientific. Amyloid-β peptide (1−42) was purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. 4’,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) was purchased from Dalian Meilun Biotech Co., Ltd. 4% paraformaldehyde was purchased from Biosharp (Anhui, China). Xanthine, xanthine oxidase, 1,1’-dioctadecyl-3,3,3’,3’-tetramethylindodicarbocyanine perchlorate (DID), 2’,7’-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), and 5,5’,6,6’-tetrachloro-1,1’,3,3’-tetraethyl-imidacarbocyanine iodide (JC-1) were provided by MedChemExpress (MCE).

Macrophage membranes (RAW) were prepared according to the reported methods of hypotonicity and freeze-thaw [31]. RAW264.7 cells were cultured at 37°C and 5% CO2 using high-glucose DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin/streptomycin. Cells were collected and suspended in hypotonic lysis buffer. The cells were then cooled in an ice bath for 30 min, and freeze-thaw cycles were repeated 5 times in liquid nitrogen to ensure effective rupture of the cell membrane and release of membrane proteins. The frozen-thawed cell suspension was centrifuged at 850 g for 15 min at 4°C to remove the precipitate. The supernatant was further centrifuged at 18,000 g for 60 min at 4°C. Finally, the precipitate was collected and sonicated under an ice bath for 5 min to obtain the purified RAW.

Solvothermal synthesis of carbon-nitrogen quantum dots. 1 g citric acid (carbon source) and 2 g urea (nitrogen source) were dissolved in 10 mL of DMF. The reaction solution was loaded into a 25 mL reactor and the reaction was carried out at 160℃ for 6 h. The obtained product was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 30 min to remove precipitates of large particles. Next, the product was dialyzed until the exudate became colorless to remove ions and small particles. Finally, the products were washed several times with ethanol and pure water to remove the remaining reactants and impurities to obtain pure carbon-nitrogen quantum dots (CQD).

Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (10 mM) was first added to RAW (10 mg/mL) and kept stable at 37°C for 1 h to allow Ce3+ to adsorb to the surface of the cell membrane (Ce3+-RAW). The solution was filtered several times at 4500 g for 15 min using a 3KD ultrafiltration tube (Amicon® Ult-4, Millipore Co., Germany) to remove unadsorbed Ce3+. Finally, the precipitate was resuspended with a solution of HEPES (25 mM) at pH 7.4 overnight at 4°C, during which Ce3+ crystallizes on the RAW surface to form Ce-RAW.

Ce-RAW-modified CQD (CQD-Ce-RAW) was prepared by ultrasonic incubation. CQD was mixed with Ce-Ce-RAW at a certain mass ratio and subjected to ice-bath sonication (200 W) for 3 min. The cell membrane on the CQD was remodeled by the energy generated from ultrasonic waves. Then, the mixed solution was shaken at 37°C for 12 h in a thermostatic vessel to obtain the CQD-Ce-RAW solution.

Nanomaterial morphology was characterized using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) from Thermo Fisher, USA (Talos F200X G2). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential were measured using a multi-angle particle size potential analyzer (NanoBrook Omni, Brookhaven, New York, USA). Scanning infrared absorption spectroscopy was performed using a Nicolet Avatar-370 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were determined using a Shimadzu UV-2550. Elemental composition and valence states were analyzed using a multifunctional X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ESCALAB QXi, Thermo Fisher, USA).

CQD-Ce-RAW was configured into solutions of various concentrations (0.05, 0.1, 0.2 mg/mL) and exposed to an 808 nm laser (2 W/cm2) for 10 min. The photothermal response of CQD-Ce-RAW at different concentrations was measured by using NIR thermal imager to monitor the temperature change. To evaluate the photothermal stability of the 0.2 mg/mL CQD-Ce-RAW solution, it was irradiated with a laser for 15 min and then cooled naturally to room temperature. The temperature was recorded every minute and the on-off process was cycled 5 times. The photothermal conversion efficiency ( η ) of CQD-Ce-RAW was determined in agreement with previously reported literature and calculated according to Eq. (1) [32].
(1)
η =
hA
T
Max
−
T
Surr
−
Q
Dis
I
1 −
10
−
A
808
Here h and A represent the heat transfer coefficient and the surface area of the container, respectively, obtained from Eq. (2).
T
Max
is the temperature of the CQD-Ce-RAW after laser irradiation, and
T
Surr
is the room temperature.
Q
Dis
is the heat dissipation of the system. I is the laser power, and
A
808
is the absorbance of the CQD-Ce-RAW at 808 nm.
(2)
τ
s
=
m
D
c
D
hA
Here
τ
s
is the time constant, which could be determined by linear regression of time in cooling period versus lnθ.
m
D
and
c
D
are the mass (0.1 g) and the heat capacity (4.2 J g−1), respectively.

The presence of ·OH was detected using the salicylic acid (SA) method. First, 1.8 mM FeSO4 was mixed with 10 mM H2O2 to initiate the Fenton reaction, which resulted in ·OH. Next, we introduced CQD-Ce-RAW into the solution to allow for a complete reaction with ·OH, a process that lasted for 2 h. Then, 1.8 mM SA was added to the solution. Since SA can react with ·OH, we used UV–vis spectrophotometry to monitor the generation of the relevant product, thereby assessing the ability of CQD-Ce-RAW to scavenge ·OH.

The reaction of terephthalic acid (TA) with H2O2 produces 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid with fluorescence characteristics. The fluorescence intensity of the product at 425 nm was detected to evaluate the ability of CQD-Ce-RAW to degrade H2O2. The CQD-Ce-RAW solution was fully reacted with H2O2 for 6 h, and then 2.5 mM TA was added for 2 h. To exclude the influence of the fluorescence properties of the material itself, the solution was filtered (13,000 rpm, 15 min) using a 3KD ultrafiltration tube (Amicon® Ult-4, Millipore Co., Germany). The fluorescence of the lower clear solution was scanned using a fluorescence spectrometer (Ex: 320 nm, Em: 425 nm).

The superoxide anion (O2-) was produced by adequately reacting 0.6 mM xanthine with 0.1 U/mL xanthine oxidase. Subsequently, CQD-Ce-RAW was added to the reaction solution and incubated for 40 min. Finally, 0.5 mg/mL HE was added, which reacted with O2- to produce a fluorescent product with a characteristic absorbance at 610 nm. The SOD-like activity of CQD-Ce-RAW could be determined by detecting the fluorescence signal of the reaction solution (Ex: 470 nm, Em: 610 nm). Afterwards, the oxidation of Ce3+ to Ce4+ by H2O2 was utilized to investigate the relationship between the catalytic process and valence conversion·H2O2 (0.2 M) was added to CQD-Ce-RAW, and the fluorescence detection was performed again after thorough mixing.

Atomic absorption spectroscopy was used to assess the ability to capture Cu2+. Firstly, a concentration gradient of Cu2+ solution (1–5 μg/mL) was configured to generate a standard curve. Subsequently, CQD and CQD-Ce-RAW (4 μg/mL) were mixed with Cu2+ (A) in a 1:1 mass ratio, and the solution was filtered using an ultrafiltration tube after 30 min of reaction. The concentration of remaining Cu2+ (B) was obtained by measuring the absorbance of the filtrate using an atomic absorption spectrophotometer (TAS-990 F, PERSEE, China). Finally, the chelation rate of Cu2+ was calculated according to Eq. (3):
(3)
Chelation rate =
A − B
A
× 100 %

After mixing 0.15 mM Cu2+ solution of 10 μM Aβ42 monomer solution, 50 μg/mL CQD-Ce-RAW was added and co-incubated for 7 d. The same volume of the mixture was removed daily and 10 μM of ThT was added for fluorescent staining. The effect of CQD-Ce-RAW on the participation of Cu2+ in Aβ42 aggregation was investigated by monitoring the fluorescence spectra for 7 consecutive days.

Subsequently, we intensively investigated the effect of CQD-Ce-RAW on preformed Aβ42 fibers. First, to obtain Aβ42 aggregates, we diluted the Aβ42 monomer solution to 10 μM and then incubated it at 37°C for 7 d to induce self-aggregation to form fiber structures. After this, CQD-Ce-RAW was added and the solution was irradiated with an 808 nm laser at 2 W/cm2 for 5 min. After continuing the incubation for 48 h, the fluorescence signals of the samples were monitored by ThT labeling (Ex: 444 nm, Em: 482 nm). In addition, Aβ42 aggregation kinetics were observed under invert microscope (ECLIPSE Ti, Nikon, Japan) and TEM.

SH-SY5Y and bEnd.3 were placed in 96-well plates, respectively. Various concentrations (0, 5, 10, 20, 40, and 50 μg/mL) of CQD-Ce-RAW were added and irradiated with a NIR laser at 808 nm (1.5 W/cm2, 5 min). After 24 h of incubation, 1 mg/mL of MTT was added to each well. The supernatant was removed and 100 μL of DMSO was added to dissolve the crystal products. Absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader (800TS, BioTek, USA) to calculate cell viability.

DID was selected for fluorescent labeling of macrophage membranes in CQD-Ce-RAW (Ex: 646 nm, Em: 663 nm). SH-SY5Y was inoculated in cell culture dishes overnight and DID-CQD-Ce-RAW (40 μg/mL) was added. After incubation for 2 h, the cells were treated with paraformaldehyde (4%) fixation for 30 min. Subsequently, 1 mg/mL of DAPI was added to label the nuclei (Ex: 364 nm, Em: 454 nm). After washing with PBS, CLSM (Leica TCS SP5, Germany) was used to observe and photograph the cellular uptake of CQD-Ce-RAW.

Changes in intracellular ROS levels were detected using DCFH-DA, which enters the cells and is oxidized to produce green fluorescence. SH-SY5Y was cultured in 6-well plates overnight. In addition to the control group, 10 μM of Aβ42 fibers were added to induce the onset of oxidative stress. Subsequently, 40 μg/mL of CQD-RAW or CQD-Ce-RAW was added to react fully with the cells for 6 h to ensure uptake and action. To assess the ROS scavenging ability of CQD-Ce-RAW under irradiation, the cells were irradiated with 808 nm laser (1.5 W/cm2) for 5 min. Staining was performed using 10 μM DCFH-DA for 30 min, and then the excess dye was washed with serum-free medium. Finally, the fluorescence signal intensity was observed under an inverted microscope (Ex:485 nm, Em:530 nm).

SH-SY5Y was inoculated in 96-well plates and treated with different concentrations of Aβ42. MTT assay was used to assess cell viability and thus screen the experimental concentration of Aβ42. Another SH-SY5Y-adhered 96-well plate was taken and 100 μL of mixed solution (CQD-Ce-RAW, 10 μM Aβ42 monomer, and 0.15 mM Cu2+) was added for co-culture. After 24 h of incubation, cell viability was measured again using the MTT assay to investigate the inhibitory effect of CQD-Ce-RAW on Aβ42 aggregation-promoted apoptosis.

In addition, the synergistic effect of CQD-Ce-RAW and PTT on the cytotoxicity induced by Aβ42 fibrils was further investigated. Aβ42 fibrils (10 μM) and CQD-Ce-RAW (40 μg/mL) were mixed and then incubated with SH-SY5Y cells. NIR laser irradiation (1.5 W/cm2, 5 min) was used or not during cell treatment. After 24 h, MTT solution was added and its light absorption value was measured to calculate the cell viability of the cells.

SH-SY5Y cells were seeded in 6-well plates and treated with CQD-Ce-RAW and Aβ42 fibers with or without NIR laser irradiation (1.5 W/cm2, 5 min). 24 h later, to reflect the functional and metabolic state of mitochondria, the cells were stained with 5 μg/mL of JC-1 for 30 min. Subsequently, the cells were washed with PBS, and the fluorescence of JC-1 monomers (green) and JC-1 aggregates (red) was visualized using microscopy (J-monomer, Ex: 514 nm, Em: 529 nm; J-aggregates, Ex: 585 nm, Em: 590 nm).

To investigate the ability of CQD-Ce-RAW to cross the BBB and be taken up by nerve cells, we established a transwell model in vitro. First, bEnd.3 cells were cultured in the upper chamber of the transwell plate, and then SH-SY5Y cells were seeded with the lower chamber. The whole model was placed in an incubator and the experiment was started after the cells had grown to the extent of 80%. DID labeled CQD-Ce-RAW was added to the upper chamber, and the cells in the light group were exposed to the NIR laser. Then, after 12 h of co-culture at 37°C, SH-SY5Y cell images were obtained using invert microscope.

First, mouse whole blood samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min. 1× PBS was added to the precipitate and washed by centrifugation (3800 rpm, 5 min). The obtained erythrocytes were reconditioned into a 5% concentration suspension. Next, different concentrations (0, 25, 50, and 100 μg/mL) of CQD-Ce-RAW were added to the suspension and mixed well. All samples were placed at 37°C for 2 h of static incubation. A mixture of pure water was used as a positive control group, while a mixture of PBS was used as a negative control group. After completion of incubation, all samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min. Subsequently, the supernatant was collected and its absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader to quantify the degree of hemolysis. The hemolysis rate was calculated using Eq. (4) [33]:
(4)
H =
OD
test
−
OD
nc
OD
pc
−
OD
nc
× 100 %

OD
test
,
OD
nc
, and
OD
pc
represent the absorbance values of the sample, negative control, and positive control, respectively.

All animal experimental operations have been approved by the Committee of Experimental Animal Breeding and Use of Jiangsu University. Balb/c mice (6 weeks old) were randomly divided into three treatment groups (n = 3): PBS group, CQD-Ce-RAW group, and CQD-Ce-RAW+NIR group. After fluorescently labeled with DID, CQD-Ce-RAW was injected into mice through the tail vein. NIR light (808 nm, 1.5 W/cm2) was used to irradiate the head of the mice for 5 min at different time points (0.5, 1, 2, 4 h) after administration. At the same time, the temperature was observed and recorded by NIR thermal imager. After 12 h of injection, the mice were euthanized and major organs (brain, heart, liver, spleen, lungs, kidneys) were dissected. The accumulation of CQD-Ce-RAW into the brain as well as its biodistribution was observed by obtaining optical images using the In-Vivo Xtreme II imaging system (Bruker).

Data were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). To determine the observed differences between any group, statistical analysis was performed using variance. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001 were considered statistically significant differences, respectively.

CQD-Ce-RAW was synthesized by wrapping CQD with Ce-RAW. First, Fig. 1a shows that the CQD prepared by solvothermal method were spherical with a diameter of about 5 nm. Vesicular nanoscale RAW was obtained under sonication conditions (Fig. 1b). The morphology of CQD-Ce-RAW was observed under HRTEM, and the light-colored RAW was seen to be distributed with dark-colored CQD in Fig. 1c. The high magnification image shows that the CeO2 crystals grown in situ on RAW have small size characteristics with a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm (Fig. 1d). In addition, the presence of Ce and N in the TEM-mapping images demonstrated the presence of CeO2 as well as the successful encapsulation of the CQD, consistent with the proposed structural model (Fig. S1). The zeta potential of the CQD was −24.9 ± 1.7 mV, which was modified by Ce-RAW to −19.3 ± 1.3 mV (Fig. 1e). In the DLS assay results, the diameter of CQD-Ce-RAW was weakly increased to 195.4 ± 4.2 nm with a PDI of 0.111 compared with Ce-RAW (Fig. 1f). Combined with Fig. S2, this indicates that CQD-Ce-RAW has better dispersion and uniform size. Notably, CQD-Ce-RAW was dispersed in PBS and 10% DMEM with tiny fluctuations in particle size over five days (Fig. 1g), suggesting excellent stability. FT-IR spectra were utilized to gain insight into the structure of CQD. In Fig. 1h, the nitric-hydrogen bond (N–H) related peak and the stretching vibrational bands of the carbonyl group (CO) appeared at 3404 cm−1 and 1624 cm−1, respectively, and the vibration of the nitrogen-carbon bonding (C–N) appeared at 1354 cm−1. This further demonstrates the doping of N in CQD and the presence of N–H providing structural support for chelating Cu2+. In addition, the UV-Vis-NIR absorption spectra of CQD and CQD-Ce-RAW have broad absorption in the near-infrared one region, implying the potential for warming under NIR laser irradiation (Fig. 1i). The Ce 3d, O 1 s, N 1 s, and C 1 s peaks are shown in Fig. S3. The presence of the +3 and +4 valence states of cerium is demonstrated in the Ce 3d spectrum (Fig. 1j).

To promote the thermal decomposition of Aβ42 aggregates, the photothermal activity of CQD-Ce-RAW was explored. Using 808 nm laser irradiation for 10 min, the temperature of 0.2 mg/mL CQD-Ce-RAW solution increased from 25.2°C to 58°C, whereas the control group only changed by 6.6°C ( Fig. 2a), implying an efficient conversion of light energy to thermal energy. It is worth emphasizing that the temperature of CQD-Ce-RAW did not change significantly after five "on-off" irradiation cycles (Fig. 2b). It demonstrated superior photothermal stability, which provides a reliable basis for long-term treatment. Based on the time constant τs = 257.8 in Fig. 2c, the photothermal conversion efficiency of CQD-Ce-RAW was calculated to be 37%. The above results demonstrate that CQD-Ce-RAW is a promising photothermal agent, providing strong support for its application in Aβ therapy.

Oxidative stress is common in the brain tissues of AD patients, so scavenging the overproduced ROS (e.g., ·OH, H2O2, O2-, etc.) is a necessary step in the treatment. Cerium in CeO2 is a mixed valence state, which is interconverted between Ce3+ and Ce4+ and thus has a redox capacity. Therefore, the multiple antioxidant enzyme activities of CQD-Ce-RAW were intensively investigated. Firstly, the ability to eliminate ·OH was investigated. Compared to the control group, no significant variation was observed in the CQD-RAW group. However, the addition of CQD-Ce-RAW resulted in a substantial decrease in the UV characteristic absorption peak at 510 nm ( Fig. 3a), highlighting its ability to remove ·OH. Subsequently, CAT-like enzyme activity was confirmed by monitoring the change in absorbance at 425 nm. The results showed that the effect of CQD-RAW on H2O2 was negligible, whereas the fluorescence value was significantly decreased in the CQD-Ce-RAW group (Fig. 3b). The above phenomenon indicates that CeO2 on the membrane exerted a strong H2O2 decomposition ability. In addition, the SOD-like catalytic activity of CQD-Ce-RAW was evaluated. CQD-Ce-RAW was added to a solution of O2- production catalyzed by xanthine oxidase. Fig. 3c shows that the absorbance showed a concentration-dependent decreasing trend, which reflects the high-efficiency removal of O2-. Notably, Fig. S4 shows that the color of the solution turned yellow after the addition of H2O2, indicating the oxidation of Ce3+ to Ce4+. When the fluorescence signal was detected again, it was found that the characteristic absorption peaks decreased insignificantly and the catalytic activity almost disappeared. This implies that the SOD-like enzyme activity of CeO2 is dominated by Ce3+, while the mutual transition between cerium valence states is correlated with the antioxidant capacity. These findings suggest that CQD-Ce-RAW possesses multi-enzyme-like activity, demonstrating its great potential as a ROS scavenger in AD therapy.

In the progression of AD, excess Cu2+ can bind Aβ42 proteins and accelerate their aggregation and precipitation. Based on this, Cu2+ is an essential target for anti-AD. The chelating ability of CQD-Ce-RAW against Cu2+ was explored because CQD has an amino group on its surface, that can complex with Cu2+. In Fig. 3d, the adsorption rates of CQD and CQD-Ce-RAW were 51.6 ± 2.3% and 54 ± 3%. The adsorption rate was slightly increased after modification by Ce-RAW, probably because RAW has some Cu2+ adsorption capacity. The above results demonstrated that the reactive amino group in CQD-Ce-RAW has a strong ability to bind to Cu2+ to form copper amines, which is expected to mitigate the effect of Cu2+ excess on the risk of AD occurrence as a metal ion chelator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regulation of Aβ42 decomposition and inhibition
ce:section.ce:para: Inhibition of Aβ42 monomer aggregation and promotion of fiber breakdown are key to intervene in amyloid plaque deposition, a typical pathological feature of AD. First, the ability of CQD-Ce-RAW to chelate Cu2+ to hinder Aβ42 fiber formation was explored and monitored using the THT method. In Fig. 4a, the fluorescence intensity of the Aβ42 monomer/Cu2+ group gradually increased over 7 days, and the change was greater than that of the spontaneous aggregation of Aβ42, suggesting that Cu2+ accelerated the formation of the fiber structure. Notably, the slightly decreased fluorescence values in the CQD-RAW group were attributed to the ability of macrophage membranes to adsorb Aβ42 thereby inhibiting some of the aggregation. After treatment with CQD-Ce-RAW, the Aβ42 aggregation kinetics showed a longer lag period, and the ThT fluorescence signal remained at a lower level. Considering the above results, CQD-Ce-RAW has a significant inhibitory effect on the Cu2+-involved Aβ42 fibrillogenesis, and the effect is more significant under irradiation.

Next, the decomposition of formed Aβ42 fibers by CQD-Ce-RAW under 808 nm laser irradiation was explored. In Fig. 4b, the absorbance of CQD-Ce-RAW at 483 nm after irradiation is lower than that before irradiation, which is due to the generation of thermal energy induced by the NIR laser to destroy the Aβ42 fibers, resulting in a decrease in the intensity of the ThT signal.

In addition, the process of Aβ42 fibrillation and disassembly was observed using TEM. Aβ42 monomers appeared filament-like structures after incubation with Cu2+ mixed for 3 d. In contrast, after the addition of CQD-Ce-RAW, Aβ42 still maintained the monomer shape, a result of chelated Cu2+ blocking the fibrillation (Fig. 4c). In Fig. 4d, light alone did not affect fiber morphology. When NIR was combined with CQD-Ce-RAW treatment, Aβ42 was broken down into short fragments and the fibrous structures disappeared, demonstrating the excellent effect of photothermal treatment. The observation results under the inverted microscope were similar to the TEM images (Fig. 4e,f). In summary, the combination of multiple experiments demonstrated that CQD-Ce-RAW can both inhibit Aβ42 aggregation and break down fibers under NIR induction.

Examining the cytotoxicity of CQD-Ce-RAW was paramount in the cell experiments. Cell viability was maintained above 90% after incubation with SH-SY5Y and bEnd.3, respectively (Fig. S5). Using a comparison test, there was no significant decrease in cell viability after NIR laser irradiation, indicating that no cell damage was induced ( Fig. 5a). Subsequently, cellular uptake was investigated using CLSM. The nuclei of the cells stained by DAPI showed blue color. The red fluorescent signal of DID-CQD-Ce-RAW was concentrated around the nucleus (Fig. 5b), implying internalization by neuronal cells. Based on the above, CQD-Ce-RAW showed good biosafety and intracellular uptake, providing a reliable basis for further exploration.

Excess ROS induced neuroinflammation which in turn exacerbated cellular damage. To explore the ability of CQD-Ce-RAW to mitigate oxidative stress, cells were stimulated to produce high levels of ROS using Aβ42. In Fig. 5c, the blank group showed weak fluorescence, and once Aβ fibers were added, the intensity of the signal increased significantly. In contrast to the addition of CQD-RAW, treatment with CQD-Ce-RAW resulted in weaker fluorescence, which indicates that the inhibition of ROS production is caused by CeO2 on the membrane. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity was significantly weakened even under the NIR laser, indicating the strong stability of CQD-Ce-RAW as a ROS scavenger. Taken together, CQD-Ce-RAW carries a powerful catalytic effect due to the presence of CeO2 and effectively protects cells from Aβ42-induced oxidative damage.

To evaluate the intervention effect of CQD-Ce-RAW on Aβ42-induced cytotoxicity, MTT assay was used for quantitative analysis. The results showed that when treated with 10 μM Aβ42, the cell viability was 60.7 ± 4.9% ( Fig. 6a), causing partial neuronal damage, which was the optimal experimental concentration to explore the protective mechanism of CQD-Ce-RAW. In Fig. 6b, CQD-Ce-RAW significantly rescued Aβ42 aggregation-induced toxicity in SH-SY5Y cells in a dose-dependent manner, utilizing Cu2+ chelating properties to inhibit toxic fiber formation. Under 808 nm laser, the blank group confirmed that the heating process did not negatively affect the cells. Meanwhile, CQD-Ce-RAW fully exhibited good photothermal conversion ability to achieve thermal dissociation of Aβ42 fibers, as evidenced by the increase in cell viability to 92.1 ± 1.9% (Fig. 6c). Overall, the combined application of CQD-Ce-RAW and PTT could reduce the accumulation of Aβ42 aggregates and thus protect neural progenitors.

It was shown that both Aβ42 and excess ROS are capable of causing damage to mitochondrial structure and function. Early apoptosis was assessed using JC-1. In Fig. 6d, in contrast to healthy cells showing weak green fluorescence, the Aβ42-treated group was very bright, reflecting that it induced a decrease in MMP and caused cellular dysfunction. Under CQD-Ce-RAW treatment, the proportion of cells with red fluorescence increased more in the light group compared to the unlighted group. This demonstrated an antagonistic effect on the negative effects induced by Aβ42 and improved the mitochondrial status. Meanwhile, the combined treatment strategy was more effective in protecting neurons from Aβ42-induced apoptosis.

The BBB has become a major obstacle in the treatment of neurological disorders. Previous studies have shown that 808 nm laser has good tissue penetration and can be used to deliver CQD-Ce-RAW to brain tissues [34]. First, an in vitro BBB model was established ( Fig. 7a). In Fig. 7b, a weak red fluorescence was observed in the lower cells of the unirradiated group, which was attributed to the fact that CQD-Ce-RAW possessed a nanoscale size advantage and was able to partially pass through the endothelial cells and be endocytosed by the neuronal cells. After NIR laser irradiation, the fluorescence signal in the lower chamber of the transwell plate was significantly enhanced, demonstrating that the photothermal effect promoted the opening of the BBB. This provides a new pathway for enhanced drug delivery to brain tissue.

Clinical application should ensure that adverse effects on the blood system are avoided. In Fig. S6, the hemolysis rate of different concentrations of CQD-Ce-RAW was less than 5%, proving that there is no potential toxicity to erythrocytes and it is a safe drug delivery system.

Given that CQD-Ce-RAW combined with PTT showed excellent BBB delivery ability in vitro, we conducted in-depth in vivo studies. The fluorescence images of the major organs of mice 12 h after intravenous injection are shown in Fig. S7. At the four time points of light exposure, the temperature difference in the mice’s brains gradually increased, laterally reflecting the continuous accumulation of DID-CQD-Ce-RAW in the brain (Fig. 7c). In Fig. 7d, e, the fluorescence intensity of the brain treated with NIR laser irradiation was 1.6 times higher than that of the unilluminated group, demonstrating that PTT helped to overcome the BBB and enhanced the DID-CQD-Ce-RAW delivery to the brain tissue. Thus, the synergistic strategy of nanotechnology and photothermal effects has great potential in the treatment of neurological diseases.

In summary, we have successfully achieved a compelling technological breakthrough by skillfully binding CeO2 to RAW in an in situ-grown manner and wrapping them around a CQD-based core. This innovative biomimetic nanomaterial provides a multi-pronged attack against the etiology of AD. We chose RAW with anti-phagocytic and anti-inflammatory properties as the carrier. Meanwhile, CeO2 was utilized as an antioxidant, and the excellent photothermal properties of CQD were fully exploited. With the assistance of PTT, CQD-Ce-RAW not only effectively decomposes the pre-formed Aβ42 aggregates, but also prevents further aggregation of Aβ42, restores Cu2+ homeostasis, and scavenges excess ROS, thereby reducing the occurrence of neuronal apoptosis. It is worth mentioning that under the irradiation of 808 nm laser, CQD-Ce-RAW successfully enhanced the efficiency of crossing the BBB, providing substantial support for its application in AD. Thus, this multifunctional biomimetic nanomaterial combined with PTT brings a brand new idea in the field of brain disease treatment.

Mingyuan Chi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jichun Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Meng Xie: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Lianxin Li: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yuewen Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu province (BK20201419) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M701502).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2024.113887.
